title: Furniture beeswax polish
subtitle: A soft wax polish for wood furniture with beeswax and lemon essential oil
excerpt: A traditional furniture polish made from beeswax and jojoba oil, scented with lemon essential oil. The beeswax feeds and protects bare or oiled wood, builds a soft lustre over time, and provides water resistance. Apply sparingly, allow to absorb, and buff to a low sheen. Fills a 100 ml tin.
sourceNotes: Traditional beeswax wood polish formulations: standard craft and woodworking reference (e.g. Finishes and Finishing by Bob Flexner, 2010 edition). Lemon essential oil as a traditional wood-care scent additive.

recipe.batchNotes: Double the batch for two tins. The polish keeps for years so making a larger batch is practical.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make well ahead; the polish improves with age as the beeswax stabilises fully.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 3 years or more in a sealed tin. Beeswax is highly stable; jojoba is very slow to oxidise. Discard only if the polish develops a rancid smell, which is unlikely within this timeframe.

glossaryTerms.definition: Beeswax penetrates slightly into the surface of bare or oiled wood and, once buffed, leaves a thin film that resists water and dust. Applied repeatedly over months and years, it builds a protective layer and a soft low-sheen lustre. It is not suitable for surfaces that need a hard, durable finish (lacquer or polyurethane), but is appropriate for antique furniture, chopping boards sealed with oil, and any wood you want to maintain with natural products.

Apply the polish in a very thin layer; too much wax applied at once will not absorb fully and will smear rather than buff. The carrier oil (jojoba) helps the wax penetrate the wood surface. Leave for at least 20 minutes before buffing; the wax needs time to begin absorbing before the excess can be removed.

content.text: A soft beeswax polish is one of the oldest wood-care products.

does not fill scratches or repair damage, but it feeds dry wood, provides a surface-level water barrier, and builds a soft lustre over repeated applications. Jojoba oil thins the wax to a soft, workable consistency without the petroleum solvents found in commercial paste wax. Lemon essential oil adds the traditional lemon-wax scent.
Use very little at a time.

into wood is limited; excess wax that sits on the surface smears and attracts dust. A thin application that absorbs fully is more effective than a heavy coat that must be buffed away. This polish is suitable for bare wood, oiled wood, and antiques. Do not use on surfaces treated with polyurethane, lacquer, or varnish; the wax will not bond and will leave a smeared surface.

items.prepNote: Cosmetic or craft-grade beeswax pellets. Yellow beeswax gives a warmer, honey-tinted polish.
Thins the beeswax to a soft, spreadable consistency. Jojoba is very stable and slow to oxidise.

5% of base weight. Add once the blend has cooled to below 40 degrees Celsius. Lemon EO has a short shelf life compared to the wax; the scent will fade after 6 to 12 months, but the polish remains effective.

content.text: Melt the beeswax in jojoba oil

Weigh the beeswax pellets and jojoba oil into the heatproof metal bowl. Set over a pan of simmering water and stir until the beeswax pellets are fully melted and the blend is clear. Remove from the heat.

Allow the blend to cool to below 40 degrees Celsius before stirring in the lemon essential oil. Pour into the salve tin while still liquid and leave undisturbed for 1 hour to set. The finished polish will be a soft, pale-yellow wax.

Apply a pea-sized amount to a clean soft cloth. Rub the cloth over the wood in the direction of the grain, applying a very thin, even layer. Leave for 20 to 30 minutes, then buff with a clean dry cloth using firm circular strokes until the surface is smooth and the sheen is even. Repeat monthly for best results.

Store in the sealed tin at room temperature. Beeswax and jojoba oil are both highly stable; the polish will keep for 3 years or more. The lemon essential oil scent will fade after 12 months but the wood-care properties are unaffected. Discard only if the polish develops a rancid smell.
